rows: Consumer right to know what PI is collected, used, and disclosed.
Disclose categories and specific pieces of PI at or before collection.
No additional PI categories without new notice.
Consumer right to request deletion of collected PI.
Direct service providers and contractors to delete PI.
Consumer right to request correction of inaccurate PI.
Consumer may request categories collected, sources, purpose, categories shared, specific pieces.
Disclose upon request: categories, sources, purposes, third parties, specific pieces.
Consumer right to know what PI is sold or shared and to whom.
Disclose categories sold/shared and categories of third parties.
Consumer right to direct business not to sell or share PI.
Provide 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link on homepage.
Wait 12 months before requesting consent to sell after opt-out.
Consumer right to limit use/disclosure of sensitive PI.
Provide 'Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information' link.
No discrimination for exercising CCPA rights.
Financial incentives for PI collection/sale only with opt-in consent.
At least two request methods (toll-free number and website).
Respond to verifiable requests within 45 days; may extend 45 more with notice.
Establish identity verification methods for consumer requests.
Clear opt-out link; treat opt-out preference signals as valid.
Respect Global Privacy Control (GPC) and similar signals.
Properly classify business and understand threshold applicability.
PI includes identifiers, commercial data, internet activity, geolocation, inferences.
Service provider processes PI on behalf of business per written contract.
Understand CCPA exemptions: employee data, B2B, de-identified data.
Private right of action for breaches from failure to maintain reasonable security.
CPPA enforcement; penalties up to $2,500/violation or $7,500/intentional violation.
Annual cybersecurity audit for businesses with significant-risk processing.
Regular risk assessments for processing presenting significant consumer privacy risk.
CPPA may audit and require risk assessment reports.
PI shall not be used for undisclosed incompatible purposes.
Automated decision-making includes decisions producing legal/significant effects.
Regulations for access/opt-out rights for automated decision-making.
Privacy policy must disclose retention period per category of PI.
Privacy policy must be updated at least every 12 months.
Notify service providers/contractors to delete PI from records.
10M+ consumer businesses must compile request metrics.
Service provider contracts must restrict PI use to specified purposes.
Sharing = making PI available for cross-context behavioral advertising.
Understand CCPA interaction with GLBA, HIPAA, COPPA.

content.text: NOVATECH ANALYTICS INC. — DATA GOVERNANCE FRAMEWORK
Document Version: 2.1 | Last Updated: November 2025 | Classification: Internal — Confidential

This Data Governance Framework establishes the policies, procedures, and standards for managing data assets across NovaTech Analytics Inc. It applies to all employees, contractors, and third-party service providers who access, process, or manage NovaTech data.
1. Data Classification
NovaTech classifies data into four tiers based on sensitivity and regulatory requirements:

Tier 1 — Restricted: Highly sensitive data requiring maximum protection. Includes PII of EU data subjects, financial account data, authentication credentials, encryption keys, and health-related data. Access limited to named individuals with explicit business need. Encryption required at rest and in transit. Logging of all access events mandatory.

Tier 2 — Confidential: Sensitive business data. Includes client behavioral data, internal financial reports, strategic plans, employee records, and proprietary algorithms. Access limited to authorized teams. Encryption required at rest and in transit.

Tier 3 — Internal: Data for internal use. Includes internal communications, non-sensitive operational data, meeting notes, project documentation. Available to all employees. Standard access controls apply.
Tier 4 — Public: Data approved for public distribution. Includes marketing materials, blog content, public API docs, press releases. No access restrictions beyond publication approval.
2. Data Handling Procedures

2.1 Collection. Data collection must be authorized by a Data Owner, documented in the Data Inventory, and limited to the minimum necessary for the stated purpose. All collection points must display appropriate privacy notices.

2.2 Processing. Processing activities must be documented in the Records of Processing Activities (ROPA) register. Tier 1 data requires a DPIA before new processing. All processing must align with documented legal bases.
2.3 Storage. Data stored in approved systems with controls appropriate to classification tier. Tier 1/2 encrypted at rest (AES-256). Storage locations documented in Data Inventory.
2.4 Transmission. Encrypted channels required (TLS 1.2+ minimum). Tier 1 data outside corporate network requires Privacy team authorization. Email transmission of Tier 1 data prohibited.
2.5 Disposal. Secure disposal when no longer needed. Electronic data overwritten or cryptographically erased. Physical media cross-cut shredded. Disposal documented with date and method.
3. Access Control
3.1 Principle of Least Privilege. Access granted on minimum permissions needed. Rights reviewed quarterly.

3.2 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). Roles: Data Owner (classification/access decisions), Data Steward (quality/compliance), Data Custodian (technical controls), Data User (authorized access for specific purposes).
3.3 Authentication. MFA required for Tier 1/2 data. SSO mandatory for production systems. Service accounts use API keys/certificates; password-based service accounts prohibited.
3.4 Access Reviews. Quarterly by Data Owners. Annual comprehensive audit by InfoSec. Departing employees: access revoked within 24 hours.
4. Data Retention

4.1 Retention Schedules. Client behavioral data (Tier 2): 24 months or per DPA. Client account data (Tier 2): contract duration + 3 years. Employee records (Tier 1/2): employment + 7 years. Financial records (Tier 2): 7 years. Marketing/sales data (Tier 3): 3 years from last interaction. Server logs (Tier 3): 90 days. Backups: 30 days rolling.
4.2 Retention Reviews. Semi-annual by Data Stewards. Expired data disposed within 30 days per Section 2.5.
5. Incident Response
5.1 Classification. P1 (critical — active breach, data exfiltration confirmed), P2 (high — suspected breach/unauthorized access), P3 (medium — policy violation/near-miss), P4 (low — minor deviation).

5.2 Response Timelines. P1: containment within 1 hour, assessment within 4 hours, full investigation within 24 hours. P2: assessment within 4 hours, investigation within 48 hours. P3: assessment within 24 hours, resolution within 5 business days. P4: resolution within 10 business days.

5.3 Notification. Regulatory authorities within 72 hours for P1 incidents with personal data (GDPR Art. 33). Affected individuals without undue delay for high-risk breaches. Clients within 48 hours per DPA terms.
5.4 Post-Incident Review. Root cause analysis within 5 business days. Lessons learned documented. Remediation tracked. Annual aggregated report to executive leadership.
6. Vendor Management

6.1 Due Diligence. Privacy/security assessment before engagement. Includes: security certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001), data handling practices, sub-processor management, incident response capabilities.

6.2 Contractual Requirements. DPAs required for all vendors processing personal data. Must include: purpose limitation, confidentiality, security obligations, sub-processor restrictions, audit rights, data return/deletion, breach notification.
6.3 Ongoing Monitoring. Annual vendor risk reassessment. Continuous monitoring of critical vendors (Tier 1 data). Material changes to security/sub-processors notified within 30 days.
7. Training and Awareness
7.1 Mandatory Training. Annual data privacy/security training for all employees. Engineering: secure development and privacy by design. Customer-facing: data subject request handling.
7.2 Specialized Training. Data Owners/Stewards: advanced classification/governance. Incident response team: quarterly tabletop exercises. New hires: training within 30 days.
8. Audit and Compliance
8.1 Internal Audits. Quarterly self-assessments by Data Owners. Semi-annual comprehensive audits by Compliance. Annual framework review/update.
8.2 External Audits. Annual SOC 2 Type II by independent auditor. Annual penetration testing by qualified third party. GDPR compliance assessment by external counsel as needed.
8.3 Compliance Metrics. KPIs: data assets classified %, access review completion, training completion, incident response adherence, vendor assessment completion, DPIA completion for new processing.

rows: Personal data must be processed lawfully, fairly, and transparently.
Data collected for specified, explicit, legitimate purposes; no incompatible further processing.
Data must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary (data minimization).
Personal data must be accurate and kept up to date; inaccurate data erased or rectified.
Data kept in identifiable form no longer than necessary. Specific retention periods required.
Appropriate security including protection against unauthorized processing, loss, or damage.
Controller must demonstrate compliance with all data protection principles.
Processing requires valid legal basis: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests.
Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous.
Legitimate interests processing requires documented balancing test.
Controller must demonstrate that data subject consented to processing.
Consent request must be distinguishable, clear, plain language, separate from other matters.
Right to withdraw consent at any time; withdrawal must be as easy as giving consent.
Child below 16 requires parental authorization for information society services.
Processing of special category data prohibited unless explicit exception applies.
Information to data subjects must be concise, transparent, intelligible, easily accessible.
Respond to data subject requests without undue delay, within one month.
Controller identity and contact details must be provided at data collection.
DPO contact details must be provided where applicable.
Purposes and legal basis for processing must be communicated at collection.
Legitimate interests pursued must be disclosed.
Recipients or categories of recipients must be disclosed.
Intention to transfer to third country and safeguards must be communicated.
Retention period or criteria for determining it must be provided.
Data subjects informed of rights: access, rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, portability.
Right to withdraw consent at any time must be stated.
Right to lodge complaint with supervisory authority must be communicated.
Whether data provision is statutory/contractual and consequences of failure to provide.
Existence of automated decision-making/profiling, logic, significance, and consequences must be disclosed.
When data not collected directly, same Art. 13 info must be provided within max 1 month.
Source of personal data and whether from publicly accessible sources must be disclosed.
Right to obtain confirmation of processing and access to personal data.
Must provide copy of personal data free of charge for first request.
Right to rectification of inaccurate personal data without undue delay.
Right to erasure when data no longer necessary or consent withdrawn.
Must inform other processors of erasure requests when data made public.
Right to restrict processing in specified circumstances.
Right to receive data in structured, commonly used, machine-readable format.
Right to transmit data to another controller without hindrance.
Right to object to processing based on legitimate interests or public task.
Right to object to direct marketing must be explicitly offered.
Right not to be subject to solely automated decisions with legal/significant effects.
Right to human intervention, express views, and contest automated decisions.
Implement appropriate measures to ensure and demonstrate GDPR compliance.
Data protection by design: implement measures at time of determination of means.
Data protection by default: only necessary data processed by default.
Joint controllers must determine respective responsibilities transparently.
Use only processors with sufficient guarantees of appropriate measures.
Processing by processor governed by binding contract specifying subject-matter and purpose.
No sub-processor engagement without prior written authorization.
Maintain records of processing activities: purposes, categories, recipients, transfers, retention.
Regularly test, assess, evaluate effectiveness of security measures.
Persons with data access must process only on instructions.
Notify supervisory authority of breach within 72 hours.
Notification must describe breach nature, DPO contact, consequences, measures taken.
Communicate high-risk breach to data subjects without undue delay.
DPIA required where processing likely results in high risk to rights and freedoms.
DPIA required for systematic extensive evaluation based on automated processing.
DPIA must contain description, necessity assessment, risk assessment, mitigation measures.
Designate DPO where core activities involve systematic monitoring at scale.
DPO contact details must be published and communicated to supervisory authority.
Third-country transfers only if Chapter V conditions are met.
Transfers permitted to countries with EU adequacy decision.
Without adequacy, transfers only with appropriate safeguards (SCCs, BCRs).
Standard Contractual Clauses are a valid transfer mechanism.
BCRs must be approved by competent supervisory authority.
If not acting on request, inform of reasons and complaint right within one month.
Notify each recipient of rectification/erasure/restriction unless impossible.
Data subject communication not required if appropriate protections render data unintelligible.
Consult supervisory authority prior to processing where DPIA indicates unmitigable high risk.
DPO must not be penalized for performing tasks; reports to highest management.
DPO tasks: inform/advise, monitor compliance, advise on DPIA, cooperate with SA.
Encourage codes of conduct for proper GDPR application.
Derogation: transfer with explicit consent after informing of risks.
Processor must assist with breach notification, DPIA, and prior consultation.
Processor must allow audits and provide compliance information.
Processor must maintain records of processing on behalf of controller.
Review DPIA when there is a change in processing risk.
Approved certification may demonstrate data protection by design/default.
Inform of right to lodge complaint with supervisory authority.

content.text: NOVATECH ANALYTICS INC. — PRIVACY POLICY
Version 3.2 | Effective Date: January 15, 2026

NovaTech Analytics Inc. ("NovaTech," "we," "us," or "our") operates a business-to-business software-as-a-service platform that enables enterprise clients to analyze customer behavioral data for product optimization, user experience improvement, and business intelligence purposes. This Privacy Policy describes how we collect, use, disclose, and protect personal data in connection with our services and website.
NovaTech is headquartered at 500 Market Street, Suite 1200, San Francisco, CA 94105, United States. We serve clients globally, including within the European Union and European Economic Area.
1. Data Collection

1.1 Data We Collect Directly. We collect information you provide when you create an account, subscribe to our services, or contact our support team. This includes name, email address, job title, company name, billing information, and communication preferences.

1.2 Data Collected Through Our Platform. When our clients integrate our analytics SDK or tracking code, we process end-user behavioral data on behalf of our clients. This may include: page views and navigation paths, click events and interaction patterns, session duration and frequency, device type, browser, and operating system, approximate geolocation (city-level) derived from IP addresses, and custom events defined by the client.

1.3 Data Collected Automatically. Our website and platform automatically collect technical data including IP addresses, browser type and version, operating system, referring URLs, pages visited, and timestamps.
1.4 Data from Third Parties. We may receive business contact information from data enrichment providers, marketing partners, and publicly available sources to support our sales and marketing efforts.
2. Use of Data

2.1 We use personal data to: (a) provide, maintain, and improve our analytics platform; (b) process transactions and send related information; (c) send promotional communications (with opt-out available); (d) respond to inquiries and provide customer support; (e) monitor and analyze usage trends; (f) detect, prevent, and address technical issues and security threats; and (g) comply with legal obligations.

2.2 Legal Bases for Processing. We process personal data based on the following legal grounds: performance of a contract, legitimate business interests, and compliance with legal obligations. Where required, we may also rely on consent.

2.3 Behavioral Analytics Processing. Our platform processes behavioral data to generate aggregated insights, trend analyses, funnel visualizations, and cohort reports for our clients. Individual-level data is retained only as necessary to provide these analytics services.
3. Data Sharing and Disclosure

3.1 Service Providers. We share personal data with third-party service providers who assist us in operating our platform, including cloud hosting providers, payment processors, email delivery services, and customer support tools. These providers are contractually obligated to protect personal data.
3.2 Business Transfers. In the event of a merger, acquisition, or sale of assets, personal data may be transferred to the acquiring entity. We will notify affected individuals of any such transfer.
3.3 Legal Requirements. We may disclose personal data when required by law, in response to valid legal process, or to protect our rights, privacy, safety, or property.
3.4 Client Data. Behavioral data processed on behalf of our clients is shared only in accordance with our Data Processing Agreement with each client and applicable law.

3.5 We do not sell personal information in the traditional sense. Our platform enables clients to analyze their own customer data; we do not sell this data to third parties for their own marketing purposes.
4. Cookies and Tracking Technologies
4.1 Our website and platform use cookies, web beacons, pixels, and similar technologies to collect information and improve your experience.

4.2 Types of Cookies: (a) Essential cookies required for platform functionality; (b) Analytics cookies that help us understand usage patterns; (c) Marketing cookies used for targeted advertising on third-party platforms; (d) Preference cookies that remember your settings.
4.3 Most browsers allow you to control cookies through their settings. Disabling cookies may limit your ability to use certain features of our platform.
5. User Rights

5.1 Depending on your jurisdiction, you may have the following rights regarding your personal data: (a) Right to access your personal data; (b) Right to correct inaccurate data; (c) Right to delete your data (subject to legal obligations); (d) Right to restrict processing; (e) Right to object to processing.
5.2 To exercise any of these rights, please contact us using the information provided in Section 11. We will respond to your request within a reasonable timeframe as required by applicable law.

5.3 California Residents. If you are a California resident, you have additional rights under the California Consumer Privacy Act, including the right to know what personal information is collected, the right to request deletion, and the right to opt out of the sale of personal information.
6. Data Retention
6.1 We retain personal data for as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes described in this Privacy Policy, unless a longer retention period is required or permitted by law.
6.2 Account data is retained for the duration of your subscription and for a reasonable period thereafter.

6.3 Behavioral data processed on behalf of clients is retained in accordance with the applicable Data Processing Agreement. Upon termination of a client agreement, we will delete or return client data within a commercially reasonable period.
7. International Data Transfers

7.1 NovaTech is headquartered in the United States. Personal data collected from individuals in the European Union, European Economic Area, or other jurisdictions may be transferred to and processed in the United States.
7.2 We take steps to ensure that personal data transferred internationally receives adequate protection consistent with applicable data protection laws.
7.3 By using our services, you acknowledge that your data may be transferred to and processed in jurisdictions with different data protection standards.
8. Security Measures

8.1 We implement industry-standard technical and organizational measures to protect personal data against unauthorized access, alteration, disclosure, or destruction. These measures include: encryption of data in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest (AES-256); network segmentation and firewall protection; regular vulnerability assessments and penetration testing; access controls based on the principle of least privilege; employee security awareness training; and incident detection and monitoring systems.
8.2 While we strive to protect personal data, no method of transmission or storage is completely secure. We cannot guarantee absolute security.
8.3 In the event of a data breach that affects your personal data, we will notify you and applicable regulatory authorities as required by law.
9. Children's Privacy
9.1 Our services are not directed to individuals under the age of 16. We do not knowingly collect personal data from children under 16.
9.2 If we become aware that we have collected personal data from a child under 16, we will take steps to delete such data promptly.
10. Changes to This Policy
10.1 We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time to reflect changes in our practices, technology, legal requirements, or other factors.

10.2 We will notify you of material changes by posting the updated policy on our website and updating the effective date. For significant changes, we may also provide notice via email or in-platform notification.
11. Contact Information

11.1 If you have questions or concerns about this Privacy Policy or our data practices, please contact us at: NovaTech Analytics Inc., 500 Market Street, Suite 1200, San Francisco, CA 94105. Email: privacy@novatech-analytics.com.
11.2 For data subject requests, please use our online form at https://www.novatech-analytics.com/privacy-requests or email dsr@novatech-analytics.com.
COMPLIANCE GAP ANALYSIS REPORT
NovaTech Analytics Inc.
GDPR & CCPA/CPRA Compliance Assessment
Prepared by: Legal & Compliance Department
Date: March 16, 2026
Classification: Confidential — Attorney Work Product
1. Executive Summary

This report presents a comprehensive compliance gap analysis for NovaTech Analytics Inc., assessing privacy practices against GDPR (82 requirements) and CCPA/CPRA (42 requirements). The assessment identified 93 compliance gaps, with 6 rated Critical and 6 rated High priority.
GDPR Assessment: 15 Compliant, 30 Partially Compliant, 31 Non-Compliant, 6 Not Applicable. Compliance rate: 19%.
CCPA/CPRA Assessment: 8 Compliant, 18 Partially Compliant, 14 Non-Compliant, 2 Not Applicable. Compliance rate: 20%.

Most significant gaps: (1) no DPO designation, (2) missing data portability rights, (3) no DPIA for behavioral analytics profiling, (4) no SCCs for EU-US transfers, (5) vague consent practices, (6) no automated decision-making disclosures. These expose the company to substantial enforcement risk, particularly GDPR fines up to 4% of global annual revenue.
Overall Compliance Risk Rating: HIGH
Immediate remediation recommended for Critical items within 30-60 days. Full program completion within 180 days.
2. Methodology
2.1 Assessment Scope
This assessment covers GDPR and CCPA/CPRA obligations. It evaluates documented policies and procedures; it does not include technical validation of implemented controls.
2.2 Documents Reviewed
2.3 Compliance Rating Scale
2.4 Priority Rating Scale
3. GDPR Compliance Matrix
Requirement-by-requirement assessment against the privacy policy and data governance framework.
4. CCPA/CPRA Compliance Matrix
Requirement-by-requirement assessment against CCPA/CPRA provisions.
5. Internal Framework Alignment
Assessment of whether NovaTech's Data Governance Framework (v2.1) supports GDPR and CCPA/CPRA compliance.
Overall: Solid foundation but gaps in DPIA, public-facing documentation, sub-processor transparency, and CCPA-specific requirements.
6. Gap Summary
20 consolidated gaps identified across GDPR and CCPA/CPRA.
Distribution: 6 Critical, 6 High, 6 Medium, 2 Low.
7. Remediation Roadmap
Prioritized action items to address identified gaps.
7.1 Implementation Phases
Phase 1 — Immediate (0-30 days)
- Designate DPO and publish contact (GAP-02).
- Add breach timeline to policy (GAP-07).
- Add retention periods to policy (GAP-08).
- Clarify controller/processor roles (GAP-12).
- Add SA complaint right (GAP-13).
- Add response timelines (GAP-16).
Phase 2 — Short-term (30-60 days)
- Implement consent management (GAP-01).
- Execute SCCs (GAP-05).
- Conduct DPIA for analytics (GAP-06).
- Deploy cookie consent (GAP-09).
- Publish sub-processor list (GAP-10).
- Implement opt-out link + GPC (GAP-11).
Phase 3 — Medium-term (60-90 days)
- Implement data portability export (GAP-03).
- Document automated decisions (GAP-04).
- Document LIAs (GAP-14).
- Implement sensitive PI limits (GAP-15).
Phase 4 — Long-term (90-180 days)
- Establish cybersecurity audit program (GAP-17).
- Implement risk assessment process (GAP-18).
- Develop recipient notification procedures (GAP-19).
- Draft prior consultation procedure (GAP-20).
8. Risk Assessment
8.1 Overall Risk Rating
Overall Compliance Risk: HIGH
Missing DPO, absent DPIA, no transfer safeguards, and vague consent create material exposure to enforcement and fines.
8.2 Likelihood/Impact Analysis
8.3 Financial Exposure
Potential financial exposure from enforcement:
8.4 Recommendations
1. Executive Sponsorship: C-level sponsorship for remediation with dedicated budget.
2. Program Management: Appoint compliance program manager across Legal, Engineering, Product.
3. DPO Appointment: Prioritize as foundational requirement enabling other compliance work.
4. Technology Investment: Consent management, privacy rights automation, data mapping tools.
5. Legal Counsel: Engage external counsel for SCCs, DPIAs, and complex remediation.
6. Monitoring: Quarterly compliance reviews to track progress and identify new gaps.
7. Board Reporting: Quarterly status updates on risk exposure and remediation progress.
— End of Report —

NOVATECH ANALYTICS INC. — DATA GOVERNANCE FRAMEWORK
Document Version: 2.1 | Last Updated: November 2025 | Classification: Internal — Confidential
This Data Governance Framework establishes the policies, procedures, and standards for managing data assets across NovaTech Analytics Inc. It applies to all employees, contractors, and third-party service providers who access, process, or manage NovaTech data.
1. Data Classification
NovaTech classifies data into four tiers based on sensitivity and regulatory requirements:
Tier 1 — Restricted: Highly sensitive data requiring maximum protection. Includes PII of EU data subjects, financial account data, authentication credentials, encryption keys, and health-related data. Access limited to named individuals with explicit business need. Encryption required at rest and in transit. Logging of all access events mandatory.
Tier 2 — Confidential: Sensitive business data. Includes client behavioral data, internal financial reports, strategic plans, employee records, and proprietary algorithms. Access limited to authorized teams. Encryption required at rest and in transit.
Tier 3 — Internal: Data for internal use. Includes internal communications, non-sensitive operational data, meeting notes, project documentation. Available to all employees. Standard access controls apply.
Tier 4 — Public: Data approved for public distribution. Includes marketing materials, blog content, public API docs, press releases. No access restrictions beyond publication approval.
2. Data Handling Procedures
2.1 Collection. Data collection must be authorized by a Data Owner, documented in the Data Inventory, and limited to the minimum necessary for the stated purpose. All collection points must display appropriate privacy notices.
2.2 Processing. Processing activities must be documented in the Records of Processing Activities (ROPA) register. Tier 1 data requires a DPIA before new processing. All processing must align with documented legal bases.
2.3 Storage

This Privacy Policy describes how we collect, use, disclose, and protect personal data in connection with our services and website.
